question: Can ROCm, Rust, and HIP materially speed up the current slow G1 residual-gate push?

followup32.assessment: A live but tiny descent was obtained. Most wall time is residual-only replay for finite-difference JVP columns and alpha candidates.

followup34.assessment: Wider local support increased runtime and did not descend, so repeated CPU support widening is not the right next acceleration path.

current_cached_jvp_speed_probe.scope: speed diagnostic only; this script uses the generic direct residual assembler and is not comparable to attached-policy residual closure

followup37_fastpath.assessment: The frame-force fastpath makes 480-column residual-only finite-difference correction cheap enough to keep exploring, but the accepted alpha remains tiny.

followup38_fastpath.assessment: The 600-column correction still descends, but the improvement tapered again; most remaining wall time is now setup and repeated candidate residual replay, not finite-difference matrix construction.

torch_rocm.assessment: Torch ROCm is installed but does not currently expose the RX 6900 XT as a cuda device, so Torch sparse/lstsq is not a usable acceleration path in this environment without runtime repair.

native_hip_smokes.assessment: Native HIP kernel execution is available and fast enough to justify a custom residual/JVP replay engine.

rust_engine_scope.current_scope: story-level nonlinear frame / hook and GPU Newton contract support
rust_engine_scope.assessment: Rust is useful as the stable FFI/orchestration layer, but the existing Rust path is not yet the full MGT frame-shell residual replay engine.

frame_internal_force.current_shape: Python loop over frame_elements with per-element 12x12 force recovery and scatter-add
frame_internal_force.acceleration_target: prepacked element arrays plus Rust/HIP element-parallel force recovery and deterministic scatter/reduction

shell_internal_force.current_shape: cached shell operator followed by CSR matvec K_shell @ u
shell_internal_force.acceleration_target: batched CSR matvec for multiple perturbed/candidate u vectors, or sparse target-row JVP extraction without full residual replay

row_correction_driver.current_shape: finite-difference support columns are evaluated sequentially through evaluate_row_candidate(), then alpha candidates are replayed sequentially
row_correction_driver.acceleration_target: batch all FD perturbations and alpha candidates through one residual replay engine and return only target rows plus residual inf metrics

implemented_followup.assessment: The first practical G1 replay acceleration is implemented for reference-geometry frame force recovery. It preserves the attached-policy direct residual at the current frontier and removes the Python frame-element loop from residual-only FD/JVP replay. Shell matvec and initial tangent assembly remain the next runtime targets.

advances.assessment: The fastpath made a 360-column element-block finite-difference row correction cheap enough to run, reopening descent beyond the prior support168 no-descent probe.
A second fastpath wide element-block pass still descended but with a smaller accepted alpha, indicating useful but tapering residual reduction.
Expanding the element-block search to 480 support columns still produced a gate-eligible descent, but the top hotspot stayed on node 2202 rx.
A 600-column follow-up remained numerically useful but tapered further, confirming that engine speed helps iteration throughput while the correction family still needs a stronger coupled direction.

latest_frontier_pattern.assessment: The same shell-bending/shell-membrane/frame cancellation frontier remains active after followup38; the faster element-block lane helps exploration but has not closed the residual gate.

decision.expected_best_use: custom HIP/Rust residual replay and batched JVP engine, not another sparse solver or Torch least-squares wrapper
decision.reason: The current accepted correction family is also algorithmically saturated. Engine throughput can shrink wall time, but the gate still needs a better coupled physical correction direction.

rough_extrapolation_from_followup32.assessment: This extrapolation is optimistic because followup34 already showed no descent; it shows why algorithmic direction and replay throughput both matter.

rough_extrapolation_from_latest_fastpath.assessment: The fastpath changes the wall-clock economics from multi-day to multi-hour iteration sweeps, but the accepted residual decrement is still far too small to rely on repetition alone.

recommended_next_path: Do not spend the next G1 push on wider CPU finite-difference support sweeps.
First build a narrow Rust/HIP residual replay prototype for the frame force-based element loop, because that is Python element-loop dominated and maps cleanly to element-parallel kernels.
Keep shell operator caching, then add batched shell CSR matvec/JVP extraction after the frame path is validated.
Modify the row-correction driver to submit a batch of support-column perturbations and alpha candidates, so one engine call replaces hundreds of Python residual-only evaluations.
In parallel, keep searching for a better G1 coupled shell-bending/membrane/frame correction direction; faster replay alone will not cure a saturated descent model.
claim_boundary: This assessment proves engine availability and identifies the acceleration target. It does not claim G1 residual closure, and the cached-JVP speed probe is diagnostic only because it does not use the attached-policy residual evaluator.
